title: Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies
author: Jared Diamond
excerpt: Why did Eurasian peoples conquer and displace Native Americans, Australians, and Africans — rather than the reverse? Jared Diamond's Pulitzer Prize-winning answer: geography and environment, not racial superiority, determined which societies developed the guns, germs, and steel that enabled conquest. A sweeping 13,000-year history tracing how domesticable plants, animals, and continental axes shaped the fate of human societies.
authorBio: Jared Mason Diamond (born September 10, 1937) is an American physiologist, evolutionary biologist, ornithologist, and author. He earned his BA from Harvard in 1958 and a PhD from Cambridge in 1961 for his work on the physiology of the gall bladder. He joined UCLA in 1968 as a professor of physiology and later became a professor of geography. Diamond's early career focused on membrane physiology and the biophysics of electrolyte transport. His second career began with the study of New Guinea birds, which led to collaboration with the indigenous New Guineans whose question inspired this book. His 1992 book The Third Chimpanzee was a bestseller and won the Rhône-Poulenc Prize. Guns, Germs, and Steel won the 1998 Pulitzer Prize for General Non-Fiction, the Phi Beta Kappa Award, and the Aventis Prize. Diamond is a member of the National Academy of Sciences, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, and the American Philosophical Society. He received the National Medal of Science in 1999 and the Tyler Prize for Environmental Achievement in 2001. A self-described cautious optimist, Diamond's work consistently argues for the primacy of environmental and geographical factors in explaining the broad patterns of human history.
